title: Bēowulf
titleEn: Beowulf — Fitt II: Twelve Winters of Grendel (lines 115–188)
part: Fitt II · Twelve Winters of Grendel (lines 115–188)
source: Fr. Klaeber, Beowulf and the Fight at Finnsburg, 1st edition (Boston: D. C. Heath & Co., 1922), pp. 5–8, lines 115–188 (fitt II). Text follows Klaeber's printing exactly — þ, ð, æ, vowel-length macrons, circumflex on contracted vowels (hēân), [brackets] for words or letters added by emendation, (parentheses) for conjectural restoration of fire-damaged letters. Pronunciation convention: reconstructed late West Saxon, c. 1000, following Campbell's Old English Grammar. Translations made fresh from the original.
about: Beowulf survives in a single manuscript, the Nowell Codex, which was scorched in a library fire in 1731; its edges have crumbled away, and some readings survive only in two transcripts made in 1787. What you read here is a reconstruction — Klaeber's edition of 1922 — and parts of it are educated guesswork: [brackets] mark letters editors added, (parentheses) mark letters restored where the manuscript is damaged or lost, and a few words are best guesses whose exact meaning no one knows. The notes say so where it matters. Line numbers are Klaeber's, the standard way of citing the poem.

sentences.t:
Gewāt ðā nēosian, syþðan niht becōm,
hēân hūses, hū hit Hring-Dene
æfter bēorþege gebūn hæfdon.
sentences.l: He went then to seek out, after night came, the high house — how the Ring-Danes after beer-taking had settled into it.
sentences.i: Then, when night had fallen, he went to see how the Ring-Danes had bedded down in the high hall after their beer.
sentences.n: The fitt opens with a verb and no name — ‘He went then…’: every listener knows who. ‘nēosian’ (‘to seek out') takes the genitive — he goes ‘of the high house'. ‘bēorþegu’ — ‘beer-taking' — says quietly that they will be sleeping deep. The circumflex in ‘hēân’ is Klaeber's mark for a contracted vowel.

sentences.t:
Fand þā ðǣr inne æþelinga gedriht
swefan æfter symble; sorge ne cūðon,
wonsceaft wera.
sentences.l: He found then therein the athelings' company sleeping after the feast; sorrow they knew not, the misery of men.
sentences.i: Inside he found the company of princes asleep after the banquet. They knew no sorrow, none of the wretchedness of men.
sentences.n: ‘sorge ne cūðon’ — ‘they knew not sorrow' — lands like a sentence being passed: in this poem, not knowing sorrow means it is already on its way.

sentences.t:
Wiht unhǣlo,
grim ond grǣdig, gearo sōna wæs,
rēoc ond rēþe, ond on ræste genam
þrītig þegna;
sentences.l: The creature of unhealth, grim and greedy, was ready at once, savage and raging, and from their rest took thirty thanes;
sentences.i: The creature of evil, grim and greedy, was ready in an instant — fierce and furious — and snatched thirty thanes from where they slept.
sentences.n: ‘unhǣlo’ — ‘un-health, un-wholeness': Grendel as the walking negative of well-being. Thirty men in one night — the scale of him is set at once, and stated with no fuss whatever.

sentences.t:
þanon eft gewāt
hūðe hrēmig tō hām faran,
mid þǣre wælfylle wīca nēosan.
sentences.l: thence back he went, booty-exultant, to fare home, with that slaughter-fill to seek out his dwellings.
sentences.i: then made off home again, gloating over his plunder, to seek his lairs with that glut of slaughter.
sentences.n: ‘hūðe hrēmig’ — ‘exulting in loot' — remember the phrase: the poem will use it again, of Beowulf leaving Heorot with Grendel's arm. ‘wælfyll’ — a ‘fill of slaughter', as if the dead were a meal; for Grendel they are.

sentences.t:
Ðā wæs on ūhtan mid ǣrdæge
Grendles gūðcræft gumum undyrne;
þā wæs æfter wiste wōp ūp āhafen,
micel morgenswēg.
sentences.l: Then in the dark-before-dawn, with early day, Grendel's war-craft was to men unhidden; then after the feasting a weeping was heaved up, a great morning-sound.
sentences.i: In the half-light before dawn, Grendel's handiwork lay plain before men. Then, where the feast had been, weeping rose up — a great cry in the morning.
sentences.n: ‘ūhta’ is the hour before daybreak — Old English kept a word for it. And hear the balance: ‘æfter wiste, wōp’ — after feasting, weeping — the two half-lines answering each other like scales tipping.

sentences.t:
Mǣre þēoden,
æþeling ǣrgōd, unblīðe sæt,
þolode ðrȳðswȳð þegnsorge drēah,
syðþan hīe þæs lāðan lāst scēawedon,
wergan gāstes;
sentences.l: The famed lord, the atheling good-from-of-old, sat unblithe; the strength-mighty one suffered, endured thane-sorrow, after they beheld the loathed one's track, the accursed ghast's;
sentences.i: The great king, that prince good from of old, sat joyless; mighty as he was, he suffered, grieving for his thanes, once they had seen the hated creature's trail.
sentences.n: ‘þegnsorg’ — ‘thane-sorrow', grief for one's men: a king's particular grief, important enough to own a compound. ‘lāst’ is a track, a footprint — they reconstruct the night's horror from what it left behind.

sentences.t:
wæs þæt gewin tō strang,
lāð ond longsum!
sentences.l: that struggle was too strong, loathsome and long-lasting!
sentences.i: The affliction was too great — hateful, and long to endure.
sentences.n: ‘lāð ond longsum’ — ‘loathsome and long-lasting' — will come back as a refrain for the Grendel years; the poem files its formulas and reuses them.

sentences.t:
Næs hit lengra fyrst,
ac ymb āne niht eft gefremede
morðbeala māre, ond nō mearn fore,
fǣhðe ond fyrene; wæs tō fæst on þām.
sentences.l: Nor was it a longer space, but about one night after he again framed more murder-bales, and mourned not for it — feud and crime; he was too fast in them.
sentences.i: Nor was there any respite: only one night later he did more murderous harm, feud and outrage both, and felt no remorse — he was too far gone in them.
sentences.n: ‘wæs tō fæst on þām’ — ‘he was too fixed in them': compulsion stated in five words. ‘morðbealu’ — ‘murder-bale': ‘morð’ is killing done in secret, the worst kind in Germanic law (the ancestor of our ‘murder').

sentences.t:
þā wæs ēaðfynde þē him elles hwǣr
gerūmlīcor ræste [sōhte],
bed æfter būrum, ðā him gebēacnod wæs,
gesægd sōðlīce sweotolan tācne
healðegnes hete;
sentences.l: Then was easy-to-find he who elsewhere, more roomily, [sought] rest for himself, a bed among the bowers, when the hall-thane's hate was beckoned to him, said soothly by a clear token;
sentences.i: It was easy then to find the man who sought his rest elsewhere, farther off — a bed out in the buildings — once the hall-visitor's hatred had been made known to him, declared truly by a token none could miss.
sentences.n: Grim comedy: attendance in the royal hall drops off sharply. ‘healðegn’ — ‘hall-thane' — is bitter irony: Grendel as Heorot's most regular retainer. ‘[sōhte]’ is supplied by editors — the manuscript lost a needed verb. The ‘clear token' is, presumably, the carnage.

sentences.t:
hēold hyne syðþan
fyr ond fæstor sē þǣm fēonde ætwand.
sentences.l: he held himself afterward farther and faster who escaped the fiend.
sentences.i: whoever had escaped the fiend kept himself farther off and safer after that.
sentences.n: ‘fyr ond fæstor’ — ‘farther and faster': ‘fast' in its old sense, firm, secure — as in ‘hold fast', ‘steadfast'.

sentences.t:
Swā rīxode ond wið rihte wan,
āna wið eallum, oð þæt īdel stōd
hūsa sēlest.
sentences.l: So he reigned, and against right he strove, one against all, until idle stood the best of houses.
sentences.i: So he ruled, warring against right, one against all, until the best of houses stood empty.
sentences.n: ‘rīxode’ — ‘he reigned': the verb of kings, handed to the monster; Grendel is Heorot's ruler now. ‘āna wið eallum’ — ‘alone against all' — has an awful grandeur; this poem lets its monsters be magnificent. ‘īdel’ — ‘idle', empty: the hall built as the seat of joy stands hollow at the height of its beauty.

sentences.l: That while was great; twelve winters' tide the Scyldings' friend suffered torment, each of woes, of wide sorrows;
sentences.i: And it lasted long: for the space of twelve winters the friend of the Scyldings endured torment, every woe there is, sorrow upon sorrow.
sentences.n: Years are counted in winters here. The manuscript writes the number as a Roman numeral (‘.XII.'); Klaeber spells it out. ‘wine Scyldinga’ — ‘friend of the Scyldings' — the affectionate royal title, now naming a helpless man; the manuscript actually garbles it (‘scyldenda’), and editors restore the familiar form.

sentences.t:
forðām [secgum] wearð,
ylda bearnum, undyrne cūð,
gyddum geōmore, þætte Grendel wan
hwīle wið Hrōþgār, hetenīðas wæg,
fyrene ond fǣhðe fela missēra,
singāle sæce;
sentences.l: therefore to [men] it became — to the children of men — unhiddenly known, sadly in songs, that Grendel strove a while against Hrothgar, waged hate-enmities, crime and feud, many half-years, continual strife;
sentences.i: and so it became openly known to men, to the children of men, mournfully in songs, that Grendel had long warred on Hrothgar — waged his campaign of hatred, crime and feud, through many a season, strife without pause.
sentences.n: The news travels as poetry — ‘known in sad songs': the poem imagining its own kind as the news medium of its world (and of course you are hearing one such song now). ‘missēra’ — half-years, seasons. ‘[secgum]’ is an editorial supplement; a word dropped out of the manuscript.

sentences.t:
sibbe ne wolde
wið manna hwone mægenes Deniga,
feorhbealo feorran, fēa þingian,
nē þǣr nǣnig witena wēnan þorfte
beorhtre bōte tō banan folmum;
sentences.l: peace he would not — with any man of the Danes' might — nor remove the life-bale, nor settle with fee; nor needed any of the counselors there expect bright compensation at the slayer's hands;
sentences.i: He wanted no peace with any man of the Danish host, would not call off the killing or compound it with payment; and none of the counselors had reason to expect bright wergild from the slayer's hands.
sentences.n: This is feud law applied to a monster. A human killer could make peace: pay wergild — ‘fēa þingian’, settle with fee; ‘bōt’, compensation. Grendel will not parley and will not pay; he stands entirely outside the legal world, and to this audience that may be his most monstrous trait of all. ‘bana’ — ‘slayer' — is the word behind our ‘bane'; the ending of ‘banan’ is restored (the manuscript reads ‘banū').

sentences.l: (but the) awesome-one was pursuing, a dark death-shadow, the seasoned and the young; he lurked and ambushed;
sentences.i: No — the fearsome one kept up the hunt, a dark death-shadow stalking veterans and youths alike; he lay in wait, he ambushed.
sentences.n: ‘ǣglǣca’ is one of the poem's most argued-over words — ‘monster', ‘awesome assailant', ‘formidable one'? Later the poem calls Beowulf himself an ǣglǣca, so ‘monster' cannot be the whole of it. ‘(ac se)’ is wholly restored — the manuscript's edge is burned away here, and Klaeber adopts a 19th-century conjecture. ‘duguð ond geogoð’ — the tried warriors and the young men: the whole warband, in a rhyme.

sentences.t:
sinnihte hēold
mistige mōras; men ne cunnon,
hwyder helrūnan hwyrftum scrīþað.
sentences.l: in ever-night he held the misty moors; men do not know whither hell-whisperers glide in their goings.
sentences.i: In endless night he held the misty moors. Men do not know where such hell-mysteries drift on their rounds.
sentences.n: ‘sinniht’ — ‘perpetual night'. ‘helrūnan’ — ‘those who know hell's secrets' (‘rūn’: rune, mystery, whispered counsel). And catch the echo: the prologue closed with ‘men ne cunnon’ — no one knows who received Scyld; the same words now shroud Grendel's goings. The poem brackets its mysteries, holy and hellish, with one formula.

sentences.l: So many of crimes the foe of mankind, the terrible lone-goer, oft framed, of hard humiliations;
sentences.i: So the enemy of mankind, that terrible solitary, committed crime upon crime, hard humiliations.
sentences.n: ‘āngengea’ — ‘the one who walks alone'. In a world where life itself means companionship in a hall, solitude is part of the horror.

sentences.t:
Heorot eardode,
sincfāge sel sweartum nihtum; —
sentences.l: Heorot he inhabited, the treasure-adorned hall, in the swart nights; —
sentences.i: He lived in Heorot itself — the jewel-bright hall — through the black nights.
sentences.n: The occupation is complete: he does not raid the hall now, he dwells in it. ‘sincfāg’ — ‘treasure-adorned': the gold still glitters; the thing living inside it has changed.

sentences.t:
nō hē þone gifstōl grētan mōste,
māþðum for Metode, nē his myne wisse. —
sentences.l: yet he might not approach the gift-seat, the treasure, because of the Measurer, nor did he know His mind. —
sentences.i: Yet the gift-throne itself he could not approach, that treasure, because of God; and of His purpose he knew nothing.
sentences.n: The most disputed lines in the poem, and honesty requires saying so. Whose throne — Hrothgar's or God's? Why can Grendel not touch it? Whose ‘myne’ (mind, desire, favor) does he not know — God's, or the throne's giver's? Every rendering is a guess; ours stays close to the bare words. The drift seems to be: some center of grace is closed to him, and he cannot even understand it.

sentences.t:
þæt wæs wrǣc micel wine Scyldinga,
mōdes brecða.
sentences.l: That was great misery for the Scyldings' friend, a breaking of heart.
sentences.i: That was deep misery for the friend of the Scyldings — heartbreak.
sentences.n: ‘mōdes brecða’ — ‘a breaking of the heart' — as close to our ‘heartbreak' as Old English comes, and used of a king.

sentences.t:
Monig oft gesæt
rīce tō rūne; rǣd eahtedon,
hwæt swīðferhðum sēlest wǣre
wið fǣrgryrum tō gefremmanne.
sentences.l: Many a mighty one oft sat at rune-counsel; they weighed rede, what for the strong-souled were best to do against the sudden-terrors.
sentences.i: Again and again the great men sat in secret council, weighing what would be best for brave men to do against these sudden horrors.
sentences.n: ‘rūn’ — counsel taken in secret; the word behind ‘rune'. ‘fǣrgryre’ — ‘sudden-terror': ‘fǣr’ is the attack that comes without warning (it survives, softened, in ‘fear'). ‘rǣd’ — ‘rede', counsel — as in unready Æthelred's nickname.

sentences.t:
Hwīlum hīe gehēton æt hærgtrafum
wīgweorþunga, wordum bǣdon,
þæt him gāstbona gēoce gefremede
wið þēodþrēaum.
sentences.l: At whiles they vowed at the heathen-shrines idol-honorings, with words prayed that the soul-slayer would frame them help against the people-calamity.
sentences.i: Sometimes they made vows at heathen shrines, offered worship to idols, and prayed aloud that the soul-slayer might send help against the nation's torment.
sentences.n: In despair the Danes turn back to the old gods — and the poet, appalled, names the object of their prayers ‘gāstbona’, the soul-slayer: the devil. ‘hærgtrafum’ — ‘shrine-tents', heathen holy places; the manuscript's ‘hrærg’ is an obvious slip, corrected by the editors.

sentences.t:
Swylc wæs þēaw hyra,
hǣþenra hyht;
sentences.l: Such was their custom, the hope of heathens;
sentences.i: Such was their way — the hope of the heathen.

sentences.t:
helle gemundon
in mōdsefan, Metod hīe ne cūþon,
dǣda Dēmend, ne wiston hīe Drihten God,
nē hīe hūru heofena Helm herian ne cūþon,
wuldres Waldend.
sentences.l: hell they minded in their mood-thought; the Measurer they knew not, the Deemer of deeds, nor wist they the Lord God, nor indeed could they praise the heavens' Helm, glory's Wielder.
sentences.i: Their hearts remembered hell; they did not know the Lord, the Judge of deeds — had never heard of the Lord God, nor learned to praise the Helm of the heavens, the Ruler of glory.
sentences.n: The poet grieves for his own pagan ancestors in a cascade of names for the God they never knew — Measurer, Judge of deeds, Lord, Helm (protector) of the heavens, Wielder of glory: variation used as lament. And a tangle the poem leaves honestly showing: elsewhere Hrothgar's court speaks of God freely; here the Danes are utter heathens. Both are in the poem, unreconciled.

sentences.t:
Wā bið þǣm ðe sceal
þurh slīðne nīð sāwle bescūfan
in fȳres fæþm, frōfre ne wēnan,
wihte gewendan!
sentences.l: Woe be to him who must through cruel malice shove his soul into the fire's embrace — expect no comfort, no whit of change!
sentences.i: Woe to him who in dire affliction must thrust his soul into the fire's embrace: no comfort to hope for, and no change, ever.
sentences.n: The poet steps forward and preaches — the sternest lines so far. ‘fȳres fæþm’ — ‘the fire's embrace': damnation as a hideous parody of shelter. Hold the word ‘fæþm’ — it returns, redeemed, two lines later.

sentences.t:
Wēl bið þǣm þe mōt
æfter dēaðdæge Drihten sēcean
ond tō Fæder fæþmum freoðo wilnian!
sentences.l: Well be to him who may after death-day seek the Lord and in the Father's embraces desire peace!
sentences.i: Well for him who after his death-day may seek the Lord, and find peace in the Father's arms!
sentences.n: ‘Wā bið… Wēl bið…’ — woe to him, well for him: the fitt closes on a preacher's antithesis, two afterlives weighed in two half-lines — and ‘fæþm’, the fire's embrace of a moment ago, is now the Father's arms. Then the sermon ends, and the nightmare goes on.
